summary: I was born in Long Prairie, Minnesota on January 30, 1946, and lived there for about six years. My dad owned a clothing store there, continuing a family tradition as a second-generation small business owner. It was a quiet start before we moved to Colorado in 1952.

My family moved to Colorado in 1952 because my dad loved mule deer hunting there and wanted to settle in a place he enjoyed. We lived in Denver, Aurora, and then Boulder until 1960. Boulder was a small, idyllic town of about 25,000 people where I grew up climbing the flatirons and living near Boulder Creek.

We lived in Denver for about a year after moving to Colorado in 1952. It was the first stop before we moved to Aurora and then Boulder. My dad was exploring business opportunities and hunting, but Denver was just a brief chapter in our Colorado years.

After Denver, we lived in Aurora for a year before settling in Boulder. Aurora was part of our early Colorado experience, but it was Boulder where we really put down roots. My dad eventually bought a motel in Boulder, and we enjoyed the natural surroundings there.

We lived in Boulder from about 1954 to 1960, on the outskirts near Boulder Creek. My dad bought a motel there, and I spent my childhood climbing the flatirons and enjoying the small-town feel of a place with only 25,000 people. Boulder was a fairly enlightened and desegregated town, which was a stark contrast to where we moved next.

We lived close to Boulder Creek when we were in Boulder, Colorado. It was part of the natural environment I enjoyed growing up, along with climbing the flatirons nearby. That area felt idyllic and peaceful during my childhood years in the 1950s.

In 1960, my family moved to Paris, Texas, which was a major culture shock after Boulder. Paris was still segregated when I attended high school there, and the racial attitudes were very different from what I had known. I went to a segregated high school and witnessed overt signs of segregation, like a sign in Greenville, Texas, nearby, proudly proclaiming 'home of the blackest soil and the whitest people.'

Driving through Greenville, Texas, on the way to Dallas, I saw a huge sign welcoming visitors as 'home of the blackest soil and the whitest people.' This sign stayed up for years after federal laws changed, showing how deeply segregation and racial pride in whiteness were embedded in the area. It was a vivid example of the environment I encountered in East Texas in the 1960s.

Although I had no formal training or friends involved in the Civil Rights Movement, I was aware of it through the news and my own experiences in segregated Texas. I reacted to my dad's motel segregation practices by secretly marking registration cards to challenge his discriminatory system. The movement's ideals influenced me even if I wasn't directly involved at the time.

In high school, I was part of folk groups that performed music inspired by the Smothers Brothers. We did a lot of their material, putting on shows and assemblies, and even funded class reunions with variety shows. Their style influenced our early music acts in Paris, Texas.

During my junior college years at Paris Junior College, we discovered Bob Dylan and other artists who electrified folk music. His music inspired us to form one of the first rock 'n' roll bands in East Texas. Dylan's influence was part of the cultural shift we experienced in the late 1960s.

After Paris Junior College, my band and I moved to Commerce, Texas, to attend East Texas State University. We kept playing music every weekend while I pursued my degree. It was a lively time balancing school and music in the late 1960s.

I played in a jug band called the 1928 Hupmobile, named after a car parked outside a popular barbecue joint in Commerce, Texas. This was a fun side project compared to my rock 'n' roll band, and it added to my musical experiences during college. It was part of the vibrant local music scene in the late 1960s.

My roommate and I performed Country Joe and the Fish's 'Fixin' to Die Rag' during our college years, which was one of the more political songs we sang. Their music connected us to the anti-war movement and the broader political climate of the late 1960s. It was part of how I got exposed to politics through music.

'Fixin' to Die Rag' by Country Joe and the Fish was a song my band performed that had strong anti-war messages. We sang it during the late 1960s, reflecting the growing opposition to the Vietnam War among young people. It was one of the few explicitly political acts I engaged in musically at the time.

Some friends from the Students for a Democratic Society (SDS) came from Austin to Commerce, Texas, to put on a political festival in 1968. They helped organize speakers and music, and despite initial hostility from local rednecks, the event ended with surprising camaraderie. SDS also provided me crucial advice about conscientious objection when I was in the Army.

Austin was the origin of the SDS activists who came to Commerce to organize the 1968 festival. Their experience and approach helped defuse tensions and make the event successful. Austin represented a more politically active and radical environment compared to East Texas at the time.

Commerce, Texas, was where I attended East Texas State University and where my band played regularly. It was also the site of a 1968 political festival organized with the help of SDS activists from Austin. Commerce was a place of both musical growth and political awakening for me during college.

I attended Paris Junior College in Paris, Texas, where most of my high school friends also went. It was during this time that we formed one of the first rock 'n' roll bands in East Texas and started playing electric instruments inspired by artists like Bob Dylan. PJC was a formative place for my early musical and social experiences.

The Vietnam War was a backdrop to much of my late 1960s experience, especially as I joined the Army in 1968. I was conflicted about going to war and eventually learned about conscientious objection through SDS contacts. The war influenced my music choices and political awareness during that turbulent time.

I went to basic training at Fort Polk, Louisiana, in September 1968, where I was older and whiter than most of the other recruits. It was a tough experience that made me realize I didn't belong in the Army or want to lead troops into Vietnam. Fort Polk was where my doubts about military service really took hold.

When I joined the Army, I was told I would serve in the Adjutant General's Corps doing easy duty at embassy parties in D.C. I believed this would keep me out of combat, but that turned out to be naive. The reality of training and deployment plans quickly made me question my decision.

Conscientious objection became a critical option for me after basic training when I realized I could not go to combat in Vietnam. A friend from SDS explained the legal path to apply for it, which was tough but possible. It was a transformative moment that gave me a way out of fighting in a war I opposed.

Canada was mentioned as an alternative for desertion during the Vietnam War era. Some soldiers considered fleeing there to avoid combat, but it was a risky choice. I discussed this option with SDS friends but ultimately pursued conscientious objection instead.
